coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of bio-inspired carbon nanodot-corn starch nanocomposite films via extrusion process for sustainable active food packaging applications

coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon nanodot (CD)-corn starch (CS) nanocomposite films are fabricated for active food packaging applications. First, ginkgo biloba leaves (GBL) were used as a biomass-derived carbon precursor, and a facile hydrothermal method was employed to synthesise environmentally sustainable CDs. The GBL-derived carbon nanodots (gCDs) were then characterised and incorporated into a CS matrix via an extrusion process to fabricate the CS/gCD nanocomposite film. The effects of various gCD concentrations on the physicochemical and functional properties of CS/gCD composite films were systematically investigated. The gCD exhibited non-cytotoxic effect against human colorectal adenocarcinoma cell line (Caco-2) cells when exposed up to 1000 μg/mL. The incorporation of gCDs into the CS film improved its mechanical properties, with the toughness of the CS/gCD2% nanocomposite film exhibiting 198 % superiority compared to the CS film. In addition, the oxygen barrier and UV-blocking properties were significantly improved. Furthermore, the CS/gCD nanocomposite film significantly extended the shelf life of ω-3 oils owing to the superior antioxidant activity of the gCDs, exhibiting only 9 meq/kg during the 15-day storage period. Our results suggest that the developed CS/gCD active composite film is a promising candidate for environmentally sustainable solutions to enhance food shelf life and reduce food waste.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of bio-inspired carbon nanodot-corn starch nanocomposite films via extrusion process for sustainable active food packaging applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon nanodot (CD)-corn starch (CS) nanocomposite films are fabricated for active food packaging applications. First, ginkgo biloba leaves (GBL) were used as a biomass-derived carbon precursor, and a facile hydrothermal method was employed to synthesise environmentally sustainable CDs. The GBL-derived carbon nanodots (gCDs) were then characterised and incorporated into a CS matrix via an extrusion process to fabricate the CS/gCD nanocomposite film. The effects of various gCD concentrations on the physicochemical and functional properties of CS/gCD composite films were systematically investigated. The gCD exhibited non-cytotoxic effect against human colorectal adenocarcinoma cell line (Caco-2) cells when exposed up to 1000 μg/mL. The incorporation of gCDs into the CS film improved its mechanical properties, with the toughness of the CS/gCD2% nanocomposite film exhibiting 198 % superiority compared to the CS film. In addition, the oxygen barrier and UV-blocking properties were significantly improved. Furthermore, the CS/gCD nanocomposite film significantly extended the shelf life of ω-3 oils owing to the superior antioxidant activity of the gCDs, exhibiting only 9 meq/kg during the 15-day storage period. Our results suggest that the developed CS/gCD active composite film is a promising candidate for environmentally sustainable solutions to enhance food shelf life and reduce food waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food packaging plays a crucial role in maintaining the quality and safety of perishable products, extending their shelf life, and ensuring safe transportation and storage. In the modern food industry, the role of packaging extends far beyond mere containment; it is a sophisticated system for preserving, protecting, and improving food quality. The evolution of packaging technologies has led to the emergence of active packaging, which actively interacts with packaged food and enhances its safety, quality, and shelf life (Ozdemir & Floros, 2004; Pereira de Abreu et al., 2012; Suppakul et al., 2003). Active food packaging systems incorporate various active agents that can control the environment within the packaging, inhibit microbial growth, and prevent oxidation (Yildirim et al., 2018).

Among the fabrication methods, the extrusion process is widely used in food packaging and processing. It has proven to be a versatile and efficient technique for manufacturing food packaging films, offering numerous advantages over conventional solution casting methods by reducing production times and exploring the possibility of industrial applications (Sakai, 2013). In addition, it facilitates the homogeneous dispersion of additives within the polymer matrix, enhancing the overall performance of the resulting films.

Corn starch (CS), a renewable and biodegradable biopolymer, has been widely explored as a promising material for food packaging owing to its environmental friendliness (Fonseca-García et al., 2021; Ghasemlou et al., 2013; B. Wang et al., 2022). However, despite their many advantages, CS films also have limitations, such as poor mechanical properties, which can hinder their use as effective packaging materials. These shortcomings have prompted researchers to explore nanotechnology to improve the performance of CS films by incorporating active compounds into the starch matrix (Gutiérrez & Alvarez, 2018; Ji et al., 2017; Kalateh-Seifari et al., 2021; Othman et al., 2021).

One intriguing approach involves the incorporation of carbon nanodots (CDs), a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial. CDs are amorphous carbon nanoparticles with quasi-spherical structures each <10 nm in size (G. Li et al., 2023; H. Li et al., 2012). CDs have shown significant potential for enhancing the mechanical properties of biopolymer films (Kong et al., 2020). Moreover, CDs exhibit exceptional antioxidant properties, making them attractive candidates for reinforcing biopolymer films (Du et al., 2020; Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023; W. Zhang et al., 2017).

Carbon precursors are crucial factors that influence the performance of CDs, offering low cost and possessing antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antiviral, immunomodulatory, and antitumor properties (Gao et al., 2023). Ginkgo biloba leaves (GBL) are rich in bioactive compounds, including flavonoids and terpenoids, that contribute to their ability to scavenge free radicals. It is known for its strong antioxidant effects and is traditionally used for its medicinal and antioxidant properties (Chan et al., 2007; Pereira et al., 2013; Singh et al., 2008). The extraction of CDs from GBL presents a sustainable approach for harnessing natural antioxidants and provides a renewable source to improve the functionality of biopolymer films. Although some GBL-derived CDs have been previously investigated as photosensitizers (Genc et al., 2020) and fluorescent sensors (Fei & Sun, 2023), the application of GBL-derived CD as natural food antioxidants in food packaging via extrusion remains to be explored.

Among the many studies conducted on CDs, research on food packaging is still in its infancy, and there have been numerous scientific inquiries regarding their behaviour. This study hypothesized that one-pot synthesised ginkgo biloba leaves-derived carbon nanodot (gCD) could enhance physicochemical and functional properties when incorporated into the CS matrix via an extrusion process, making it a compelling candidate for active food packaging material. The main objectives of this research include: 1) to synthesise CDs from GBL by hydrothermal bottom-up synthesis, 2) to incorporate gCDs into CS films via an extrusion process and evaluate various physicochemical properties, and 3) to assess the functional properties through food application tests to gain insights into their applicability in maintaining food quality as active food packaging materials.

GBL were obtained from a local market in Gangwon-do, Korea. potassium persulfate, 2,2-Diphenyl-1-bitter hydrazine (DPPH), and 2,2-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Soluble starch was bought from Yakuri Pure Chemicals (Kyoto, Japan). Corn starch (chemical pure grade, CAS #: 9005-25-8, Catalogue #: 7661-1405, amylose content: 26%, moisture content: 10.5 %, specific gravity 1.5) was purchased from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co. (Siheung, Korea). All other chemicals, including chloroform, calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2), 0.01 N sodium thiosulfate, glycerol, and potassium iodide were analytical grade and supplied from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co. (Siheung, Korea). ω-3 oil was bought from Natural Life Nutrition Inc. (Burnaby, Canada), and human colorectal adenocarcinoma cell line (Caco-2) cells were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (Rockville, MD, USA).

CDs were synthesised using GBL as a carbon precursor by hydrothermal synthesis according to a previous method with some modifications (Han et al., 2020). First, GBL were ground using a laboratory blender. Subsequently, the grounded GBL was finely ball-milled using a Retsch MM200 (Retsch, Haan, Germany) with 5-mm zirconium balls at 500 rpm for 1 h. After that, dried GBL (7 g) was mixed with 140 mL of distilled water (5 % w/v) in a polypropiolactone (PPL)-lined chamber and placed in a hydrothermal synthesis reactor, which was heated to 200 °C for 3 h. After immediate cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged at 18,000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C, followed by filtration of supernatant using a 0.20 μm membrane syringe filter (Hyundai micro, Seoul, Korea) to remove impurities. The filtrate was then freeze-dried to obtain the gCD powder and stored in a vacuum chamber before further testing.

The microstructure of the gCD was observed using field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM; Tecnai G2 F30ST, FEI Company, Hillsboro, OR, USA) to confirm the shape and size of the synthesised CD. The average particle size and size distribution were evaluated using ImageJ software. Light absorption was measured using a UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer equipped with a PbS detector (Cary 5000; Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). Fluorescence spectra were acquired using an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). The functional groups of the gCDs were analysed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Cary 630; Agilent Technologies, Inc., Santa Clara, CA, USA) in the infrared range of 4000–650cm−1. Spectra were collected in 128 scans at a resolution of 4cm−1 for each sample. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was conducted using a SmartLab (Rigaku Co., Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation in the range of 2θ = 10–40°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) analysis was performed using a PHI X-TOOL instrument (ULVAC-PHI, Kanagawa, Japan). The binding energy shift was adjusted to 284.6 eV at the C1s level.

DPPH•, a stable nitrogen-centred free radical, is commonly used for quantitative determination of antioxidant activity. DPPH• ethanol solution (0.2 mM/L) was blended with different concentrations of gCDs (100–1000 μg/mL), followed by exposure to dark. After 30 min, the UV–Vis absorbance at 517 nm was measured, and the DPPH• radical scavenging capacity was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
DPPH scavenging activity
%
=
A 0
−
A 1
/
A 0
× 100
,
where A 0 and A 1 correspond to the absorbances at 517 nm of the radical (DPPH•) in the absence and presence of antioxidants, respectively.

The ABTS assay was conducted using a slightly modified version of a previously described method (Koutamehr et al., 2023). ABTS solution was prepared via the reaction of 7.4 mM aqueous ABTS solution and 2.45 mM of potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) solution. After 16 h of storage in the dark, the radical cation solution was diluted to an initial absorbance value of 0.7 ± 0.03 at 734 nm to obtain ABTS•+ free radical working solution. The dispersion was then treated with different concentrations of gCDs (100–1000 μg/mL). After 10 min of dark incubation, the absorbance at 734 nm was measured. The antioxidant activity of gCD was calculated using Eq. (2):
(2)
ABTS scavenging activity
%
=
A c

A s
/
A c

,
where A c and A s are the absorbance values of ABTS at approximately 734 nm in the absence and presence of the gCDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and viability assay of gCD
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the gCD was measured using the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay with a slight modification from a previous study (Kim et al., 2022). Briefly, Caco-2 cells were cultured on a Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM; low glucose, Gibco, NY, USA) containing sodium bicarbonate of 3.7 g/L and 1 % (v/v) penicillin/streptomycin, along with 10 % (v/v) foetal bovine serum. Next, the cells were incubated in humidified incubator at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 concentration. After that, the Caco-2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells/mL and incubated, followed by treatment with gCD. The MTT solution was then added to the wells and left to sit for 3 h. Next, the MTT solution was removed and DMSO was added. Finally, the absorbance was measured at 540 nm using a microplate reader (EL-808; BioTek, Bad Friedrichshall, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CS/gCD nanocomposite film via twin-screw extrusion process
ce:section.ce:para: The CS/gCD nanocomposite films were fabricated using a co-rotating twin-screw extruder (HAAKE Process 11; Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Karlsruhe, Germany). The temperature for the extrusion process was set at 85, 90, 95, 105, 105, 100, 100, 95, and 95 °C from the feed to the sheet die zones. These operating parameters were selected as optimal based on our previous experience. Pure thermoplastic CS film contained CS: glycerol: distilled water = 10: 3: 2. The gCDs were added at 0.5, 1, and 2 % weight ratios to the thermoplastic starch mixture. After extrusion, the extrudate (thickness of 1.0 ± 0.04 mm) was pressed into thin films by a heat press machine (QM900S, QMESYS, Uiwang-si, Gyeonggi-do, Republic of Korea). Heat compression was performed between heated plates at 30 MPa for 90 s at 95 °C. Following extrusion and compression, the film attained a final thickness averaging 0.19 ± 0.04 mm. Final films were conditioned at 25 °C and 50 % RH before the test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
Film colour was evaluated using a colorimeter (CR-400; Konica Minolta Sensing, Inc., Chiyoda, Tokyo, Japan) calibrated on a standard white plate (Y = 94.00, x = 3.82, and y = 0.29). The films were placed on a standard white plate, and the CIE L*a*b* values were recorded at five random locations on the film surface. The colour difference (ΔE*) was calculated according to following Eq. (3):
(3)

where L*, a*, and b* are the colour values of the standard white plate, and L, a, and b are those of the film samples.
X-ray diffraction patterns of neat CS and its nanocomposite films were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (SmartLab; Rigaku Co., Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation, within a 2θ range of 5–35°.

The FTIR spectra of neat CS and its nanocomposite films were obtained and analysed using a Cary 630 FT-IR spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, Inc., Santa Clara, CA, USA) in the 4000–650cm−1 range, with 128 scans per sample at a resolution of 4cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: Film thickness (μm) was measured at five random positions on the film using a digimatic indicator with a precision of 0.001 mm (ID-C112X; Mitutoyo Corp., Kawasaki, Kanagawa, Japan). The mechanical properties of the specimens, including the tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EAB), and elastic modulus (EM), were investigated according to the ASTM D882–02 standard method using a universal testing machine (Instron 3366; Instron Engineering Corp., Norwood, MA, USA). Each specimen was prepared as a rectangular strip with dimensions of 15 mm × 100 mm and placed between the grip heads of the testing machine. The crosshead speed and initial gauge length were 50 mm/min and 50 mm, respectively. Five specimens of each film were tested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content (MC) and water solubility (WS) of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
The square-shaped films (20 mm × 20 mm) were pre-weighed (W) and dried in an oven at 105 °C for 24 h. After the initial dry matter (W1) was measured, the dried films were immersed in 50 mL of distilled water for 24 h at 25 °C. The insoluble films were taken out and re-dried at 105 °C to determine the final dry mass (W2). Five replicates were performed for each film sample and the MC and WS of the films were determined using Eq. (4) and (5), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Barrier properties of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films

ce:section.ce:para:
WVP was determined gravimetrically using ASTM E96-95. Circular cups containing anhydrous calcium chloride were used and each sample was hermetically sealed in a cup. The weights of the sealed cups were measured to a precision of 0.001 g, and the cups were placed in an environmental chamber at a controlled temperature (25 °C) and RH (50 %). The cups were weighed after 24 h and measurements were performed in triplicate. The WVP of the films was calculated using Eq. (6).
(6)
WVPgmm /
m 2
· day · kPa ) = W · x / A · T
P 2
−
P 1
,
where W is the weight gain of the cup (g) after 24 h, x is the thickness of the film (mm), A is the exposed area of the film, and (P 2 − P 1) is the difference in water vapor pressure (kPa) across the specimens.

An oxygen permeation analyser (OX-TRAN Model 2/22 H, Mocon Inc., Minneapolis, MN, USA) was used to analyse the OP of films according to the ASTM D3985 at 23 °C and 0 % RH. The OP value was calculated using Eq. (7).
(7)
OP

V × x

where V is the volume passed through the film (cm3), x is the film thickness (μm), A is the testing area of the films (m2), t is the time, and ΔP is the difference in the partial pressure across the films (atm). Three replicates were used for each experiment.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of the neat CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite film samples were acquired in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Cary 5000; Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: For TGA analysis, 5–6 mg of each film specimen was analysed using a thermogravimetric analyser (SDT Q600; TA Instruments Inc., New Castle, DE, USA) under a nitrogen atmosphere. Samples were heated from ambient to 600°C at a constant heating rate of 10°C/min. The results are plotted as weight loss versus temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Packaging of olive oil in developed pouches
ce:section.ce:para: Linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE), neat CS, and CS/gCD2% films were cut into squares (60 mm × 60 mm) and heat sealing was used to fabricate film pouches by an impulse heat sealer (H-163, Uline Inc., WI, USA). An aliquot of ω-3 oil (1.5 g) was transferred into the pouches, heat-sealed, and the film pouch samples were stored at an ambient environment of 24 ± 1 °C in a laboratory for 15 d.

The peroxide value (PV) was determined according to the previous method with slight modifications (Amiri et al., 2019). A portion of the ω-3 oil (1 g) was extracted from each packaging pouch and dissolved in 25 mL of an acetic acid and chloroform solution mixed at a 3:2 volume ratio. Following this, 1 mL of a saturated potassium iodide solution was added, and the solution was left in the dark for 10 min. Afterward, distilled water (30 mL) was added, followed by starch solution (1 mL) to act as an indicator. The resulting mixtures were titrated with 0.01 N sodium thiosulfate until they turned clear. The PV value of each sample was measured every three days and analysed in triplicate.

Data were analysed with a completely randomised design by one-way analysis of variance using the SPSS software (Statistical Package for the Social Sciences, SPSS 26.0, IBM, USA). Duncan's multiple-range test at α = 0.05 was used to evaluate the differences between means. All data are presented as mean ± SD (standard deviation (SD).

The synthesised gCD showed yellow-brownish tint (Fig. S2 (A)) and emitted blue luminescence under UV (365 nm) (Fig. S2 (B)). The TEM image indicates that the morphology of the gCD particles has a nanosized dotted shape (Fig. S2). The average size of the gCD was determined to be 6.41 ± 1.03 nm from the particle size distribution analysed by the ImageJ software program. The XRD patterns of the gCDs, shown in Fig. S3, exhibited a very broad diffraction peak from 2θ = 18–38° centring at 2θ = 20°, confirming the signature of amorphous character of elemental carbon (Siddique et al., 2020). The FT-IR spectrum reveals the presence of functional groups on the surface of the gCDs (Fig. S4). The strong and broad peak centred at 3395 cm−1 is related to the existence of -OH stretching (Lyu et al., 2019). Absorption peaks at 2924 and 675 cm−1 are associated with the stretching and bending vibrations of CH and = C–H, respectively (Kurdekar et al., 2016). The semi-wide peak centred at 2112 cm−1 is attributed to the CC stretch. The characteristic absorption peak at 1589 cm−1 is related to the presence of the CC stretching (Fei & Sun, 2023). The absorption peaks at 1387 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 of gCDs represent the CH2 and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The presence of CO stretching was also observed as sharp peak centred at 1060 cm−1 (Souza da Costa et al., 2018). The FT-IR spectrum shows multiple oxygen and carbon functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The presence of hydrophilic functional groups of the gCDs could further improve the water solubility.

The elemental composition of the gCD was investigated by XPS. As shown in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1(A)), the synthesised nanodots were primarily composed of carbon (C1s, 284.6 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 399.5 eV), and oxygen (O1s, 531.9 eV). The respective atomic composition ratios were determined as C1s (82.9 %), O1s (11.5 %), and N1s (5.6 %), showing high purity of carbon. Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 1(BD)) of single-element fitting confirmed the binding energies corresponding to the types of chemical bonds on the gCDs. XPS spectrum of C1s consisted of three characteristic peaks of the CC, CO, and CO structures located at 284.2, 285.7, and 287.6 eV, respectively. O1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(C)) contains CO (531.9 eV) and CO (530.5 eV). Additionally, a CN peak is observed at 399.4 eV in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2(D)). A comparative analysis of XPS with previously conducted FTIR analysis revealed that the manufactured gCDs predominantly exhibited an abundance of CC and CO bonds.

The photophysical properties, including the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectra, of the gCDs were analysed and are presented in Fig. 2(E) and 2(F), respectively. The absorption of the gCDs exhibited characteristic two main absorption peaks. A sharp and dominant absorption peak at 288 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bond, resulting from the absorption of the carbon atom in cyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Meanwhile, the weak shoulder peak at 313 nm indicates n- π* transition of oxygen-abundant functional groups on the surface of the gCD (Xiong et al., 2023; Q. Zhang et al., 2021). Regarding FL properties, the emission spectra of the gCD at excitation wavelengths of 270–490 nm exhibited no sharp emission peaks at excitation wavelengths of 270–290 nm. However, as the excitation wavelength was increased further, a well-defined peak was observed in the emission spectrum. The maximum FL emission was observed at 400 nm, which was excited at 310 nm. As the excitation wavelength increased from 310 to 490 nm, the emission of the gCD exhibited a redshift, gradually shifting to higher wavelengths, accompanied by a decrease in FL intensity. Consequently, these findings indicate that the synthesised gCDs exhibited a zero-dimensional spherical shape and structurally contained a carbonaceous core with abundant surface functional groups.

The in-vitro antioxidant activities of the gCDs were analysed using both the DPPH and ABTS methods at gCD concentrations ranging from 100 to 1000 μg/mL (Fig. 3 ). Firstly, the absorption of DPPH decreased as the concentration of gCD gradually increased. These results indicate that the gCDs have a high dose-dependent scavenging activity. Similarly, the antioxidant activity of the gCDs was found to be concentration-dependent ABTS•+ radical scavenging activity. A decrease in the absorbance value was confirmed with a gradual increase in gCD concentration, and the highest scavenging activity of gCD was found at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL. The superior antioxidant properties of gCD may be attributed to their ability to reduce and neutralise the free radicals ABTS• and DPPH• through hydrogen transfer from their abundant hydroxyl groups (Das et al., 2022). However, their radical scavenging activity varied depending on the method where the highest scavenging activity was recorded, which was 72.36 ± 0.41 % for DPPH and 84.20 ± 0.41 % for ABTS. The higher scavenging activity of ABTS than that of DPPH is consistent with previous results and can be attributed to the higher dispersion of gCDs in the aqueous medium used in ABTS versus the ethanolic medium used in DPPH, as the gCDs fabricated in this study have high hydrophilicity (Das et al., 2022; Roy et al., 2022).

Low cytotoxicity is one of the most important requirements for incorporating functional ingredients into food packaging materials. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of the gCDs, the viability of colon-derived Caco-2 cells was selected as a model for human intestinal epithelial cells. The relative viability of Caco-2 cells exposed to various concentrations of gCD (62.5, 125, 250, 500, and 1000 μg/mL) was measured via the MTT assay for 48 h. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the viability of Caco-2 cells at all gCD concentrations in the range of 0–1000 μg/mL was maintained above 90 % after 24 h, indicating non-cytotoxicity towards gCDs. After 48 h, cell viability showed a slight decreasing trend as the gCD concentration increased, reaching 82.30 % viability at its highest concentration (1000 μg/mL). Nonetheless, no toxic effects were observed at any gCD concentration, with cell viability remaining above 80 %. Thus, the synthesised gCDs can be used as food packaging materials owing to their high cell viability even with prolonged exposure, suggesting good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of the gCDs (Y.-Y. Chen et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of the CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films are listed in Table 1 . The CS film exhibited values of 91.19 ± 0.45, 4.46 ± 0.15, and 1.99 ± 0.21 for L*, a*, and b*, respectively, showing high brightness. On the contrary, L
⁎ of the CS/gCD nanocomposite films was significantly decreased (P < 0.05) to 83.35 ± 0.99 in CS/gCD2%; on adding gCD, the film's yellowness (b*) increased significantly to 3.69 ± 0.30, 7.99 ± 0.09, and 13.61 ± 0.69 in CS/gCD0.5%, CS/gCD1%, and CS/gCD2%, respectively. Accordingly, the ΔE value increased with the incorporation of gCD; however, the a* value did not change significantly. These results suggest that the CS/gCD nanocomposite films have a more intense dark yellow colour than the neat CS film, which is attributed to the inherent yellow-brownish colour of the gCD. This corresponds to a previous study in which the b* value of the chitosan film was increased by the inclusion of yellowish CDs (Wen et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: To further comprehend the crystalline structures, X-ray diffraction patterns of both the neat CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films were analysed within the 2θ range of 5–35° (Fig. 5 ). In the neat CS film, distinct diffraction peaks appeared around 2θ = 13°, 15.2°, 17.03°, 19.8°, and 22.44°, indicative of an A-type crystalline starch structure (L. Li et al., 2018). The peak at 2θ = 19.8° corresponds to the recrystallization of simple helical amylose molecules during cooling after extrusion, indicating the formation of Vh-type crystalline arrangement by starch amylose at high melting temperatures (Azevedo et al., 2017). Upon the addition of gCD to the neat CS film, there were no observable changes in the overall diffraction peak positions, shapes, or intensity of the diffraction peaks. These findings indicate that the incorporation of gCD did not alter the X-ray diffraction patterns of corn starch. This lack of change may be attributed to the homogeneous dispersion of gCD within the starch matrix and the low concentration of gCD, which is insufficient to alter crystallinity or cause agglomeration (Koshy et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of neat CS and its nanocomposite films are depicted in Fig. 6 . The neat CS film exhibits characteristic broad bands at 3285 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of the -OH groups in starch. Two minor peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2873 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric CH bonds, respectively. A distinctive peak at 1645 cm−1 is associated with the swelling of the starch films due to water absorption (Merci et al., 2019). The peak at 1149 cm−1 is related to the stretching of the CO bond. Additionally, a strong peak at 995 cm−1 indicates COH bending vibrations. Some changes in the spectrum were observed after the incorporation of gCD. Firstly, the peak around 3285 cm−1 in the neat CS film shifted slightly to 3263 cm−1 in CS/gCD2% nanocomposite, likely due to hydrogen bonding interactions between the -OH groups of CS and gCD (Konwar et al., 2015; Silva et al., 2024). Furthermore, the increased intensity of the peak at 1645 cm−1 suggests that the incorporation of gCD into the starch film matrix slightly enhanced the hydrophilicity of the starch film. This phenomenon aligns with the subsequent results (Section 3.8) of increased moisture content and water solubility. Consequently, the presence of gCD likely enhances the water affinity of the CS film. However, the main bands in the FTIR spectra of the gCD nanocomposite films did not show significant variations, exhibiting the typical behaviour of corn starch film. Previous research also reported that the IR spectra of solvent-casted carboxymethyl cellulose/gelatin film retained its major IR peaks with the addition of eggplant peel-derived carbon nanodot (Khan et al., 2024). This suggests that the incorporation of gCD caused microstructural changes in the CS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties of these films are listed in Table 2 . The initial tensile strength (TS) of the neat CS film was 5.20 ± 0.27 MPa, which was consistent with the previously reported starch-based film (Choi et al., 2023). The TS was significantly enhanced as the amount of gCDs increased, indicating the presence of nano-reinforcing agents in the starch film matrices. This can be attributed to the strong electrostatic interactions between the starch polymer matrix and the gCDs. Moreover, EM of the neat CS film (50.63 ± 7.85 MPa) gradually increased further to 73.35 ± 8.49, 111.48 ± 17.51, and 151.26 ± 12.45 MPa in CS/gCD0.5%, CS/gCD1%, and CS/gCD2%, respectively (P < 0.05). The mechanical strength of the CS/gCD nanocomposite films improved significantly with increasing concentration, which is in line with previous results (Zhao et al., 2022). This tendency may be related to the stiffening effect of carbon dots, which have an aromatic carbonaceous core structure (Konwar et al., 2015). Overall, the mechanical strength and stiffness of the CS/gCD nanocomposite film increased significantly by 87.31 % and 198.76 %, respectively (P < 0.05), while the EAB of the film decreased by 16.87 % in the CS/gCD2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content (MC) and water solubility (WS) of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: Water-related properties, such as MC and WS, of the CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films are presented in Fig. 7 . It is clear from these results that the overall hydrophilicity increases with the incorporation of gCDs. The initial MC of the neat CS film was 20.47 %, which continuously increased to 22.18 % for CS/gCD2% (P < 0.05). Similar behaviour was observed in WS analysis—the value of WS significantly increased from 17.23 ± 0.27 % in neat CS to 19.07 ± 0.35 % in CS/gCD2% with an increase in amount of gCDs (P < 0.05), indicating enhanced hydrophilicity. A similar result was observed in a previous study, in which the addition of nitrogen-doped CDs increased the water solubility of chitosan/polyvinylpyrrolidone films, which is attributed to the higher number of polar functional groups introduced by the CDs (Kalanidhi & Nagaraaj, 2022). Specifically, CDs typically exhibit hydrophilicity on their surfaces owing to the presence of functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The hydrophilic nature of gCDs can enhance the overall hydrophilicity of the composite materials, leading to increased MC and WS in the CS/gCD nanocomposite films. When water molecules interact with the starch composite film matrix, the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the gCDs facilitate the dissolution or dispersion of certain components, thereby potentially increasing the overall WS of the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Barrier properties of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films

ce:section.ce:para: The WVP values of CS and various CS/gCD nanocomposite films are listed in Table 3 . The WVP values of initial neat CS was 6.78 ± 0.30 g·mm/m2·day·kPa, which is similar to the behaviour exhibited in a previous study (Sun et al., 2014). The WVP of all CS/gCD nanocomposite films significantly increased (P < 0.05) compared to that of the neat CS film, with the CS/gCD2% nanocomposite film reaching 7.63 ± 0.27 g·mm/m2·day·kPa, reflecting the hydrophilicity of gCDs (Sjahriza et al., 2018). Similar behaviour was observed in a previous study, in which the addition of CDs increased the WVP of chitosan films (S. Chen et al., 2023). CDs are known for their hydrophilic nature, which means that they have an affinity for water molecules. When added to a starch film matrix, hydrophilic CDs form hydrogen bonds with water molecules, facilitating the movement of water vapor through the film. This is also supported by the increase in WS and MC, as mentioned in Section 3.8. However, the increment in WVP did not show any statistical significance to the increasing concentrations of gCD in CS (P ≥ 0.05).

The OP values of neat CS and its nanocomposite films are listed in Table 3. The initial neat CS film showed an OP value of 1337.34 ± 115.87 cm3 × μm/m2 × day×atm. However, the nanocomposite films exhibited superior barrier properties against oxygen. The OP values of all the CS/gCD nanocomposite films were significantly lower (P < 0.05) than that of the neat CS film. The incorporation of gCD into the CS film matrix significantly improved the oxygen barrier properties of the films with increasing gCD concentration, reaching 849.14 ± 70.35 cm3 × μm/m2 × day×atm in the CS/gCD2% nanocomposite film (P < 0.05). The hydrophilic nature of gCDs can deter nonpolar oxygen solubility in the CS film matrix (Panrong et al., 2020). Therefore, the addition of gCDs can potentially hindering oxygen movement through the film by increasing interaction with water molecules. Moreover, this could be attributed to the formation of a denser network through interactions between the starch molecules and CDs. This improvement in the oxygen barrier properties can be interpreted in combination with the water barrier properties (Section 3.9.1), as they show an inverse correlation.

It is a well-known fact that UV light initiates and accelerates the lipid oxidation of oils through the formation of free radicals, leading to food spoilage (Albert et al., 2013). Therefore, the UV-blocking properties of CS and its gCD-incorporated nanocomposite films were measured, and these are depicted in Fig. 8 . The UV spectra were categorically segmented into three regions: UV-C (200–280 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm), and UV-A (315–400 nm). Consequently, the UV-blocking properties of the individual film groups were investigated over the wavelength spectrum of 200–400 nm. The results showed that the neat CS film exhibited higher transmittance over the entire wavelength range than the other three groups infused with gCDs, indicating its inability to block UV light. Conversely, when different concentrations of gCDs are incorporated into CS films, they absorb UV light across all regions, which is consistent with the findings of previous studies (Barman et al., 2021). Specifically, the neat CS film allowed an average light transmission of 35.33 % in the UV-B region, whereas the incorporation of gCD significantly reduced this transmission to 13.17 %, 8.79 %, and 1.09 % in CS/gCD0.5%, CS/gCD1% and CS/gCD2%, respectively. A parallel trend was observed in the UV-A region, where the CS/gCD2% film achieved an average UV-A light blockage of 89.06 %, markedly higher than the 50.27 % blockage observed with the neat CS film. The nanocomposite film groups showed significantly better UV-blocking properties than the control group, and this trend became even more pronounced as the concentration of the added gCDs increased. Consequently, it is demonstrated that the CS/gCD nanocomposite film effectively attenuates UV-induced lipid oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: TGA measures the amount and rate of weight loss as a function of temperature under controlled conditions. The weight loss at different temperatures provides information on the composition and thermal stability of the polymer (Coats & Redfern, 1963). The thermal stabilities of the neat CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films were analysed and presented as thermograms. As shown in Fig. 9 , all films underwent a three-step weight loss. The first weight-loss step was related to the evaporation of free water. The second step is related to the decomposition of glycerol, which is used as a plasticiser and low-molecular-weight molecule. The last step is related to the oxidation of partially decomposed starch (Abera et al., 2020). When the gCDs were added to the film, a slight change in the thermal record was observed. The maximum decomposition temperature of the nanocomposite films containing gCDs decreased slightly compared with that of the control film as the proportion of gCDs increased. This decrease may be attributed to the facilitation of hydroxyl group bonding between the gCD and starch, which reduces the intermolecular bonding of the hydroxyl groups among the starch molecules. And at 600 °C, the CS/gCD nanocomposite films showed a markedly increased residual carbon content: from 5.86 % in neat CS film to 8.53 %, 9.83 %, and 11.25 % in CS/gCD0.5%, CS/gCD1%, and CS/gCD2%, respectively. This behaviour is similar to previous results, and it is noteworthy because the thermal stability of the nanocomposite film increased as high residual char protects the polymer matrix from heat flux and degradation, thereby improving its flame retardancy (Yu et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxide value (PV) of ω-3 oil packaged in CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The high susceptibility of ω-3 oil to rancidity prompted its selection for antioxidant evaluation in a food application test. The oxidative stability of ω-3 oil packed with the developed CS/gCD2% nanocomposite film was investigated for a 15-d storage period (see Fig. 10 ). Within the third day, the ω-3 oil enclosed in LDPE film rapidly became rancid and exceeded the standard value of 60 meq/kg. In addition, the PV sharply rose to 550 meq/kg within the 15-day storage period, indicating the lack of a protective barrier effect of the LDPE bag for ω-3 oil. In contrast, both the neat CS and CS/gCD nanocomposite films exhibited robust barriers against oil rancidity. Even the neat CS film did not exceed the threshold value of 60 meq/kg, possibly due to its lower oxygen permeability (OP) compared to LDPE, as indicated in our previous research (Song et al., 2022) with an OP value of 130,140 ± 1060.66 cm3∙m/m2∙day∙atm.

Compared to the neat CS, the CS/gCD2% nanocomposite film demonstrated significantly improved rancidity-inhibiting properties throughout the storage period (P < 0.05). Although the PV of the CS/gCD2% nanocomposite film increased, it remained far below the standard limit (60 mEq/kg), reaching only 6 mEq/kg by day 15. This superior performance could be attributed to the fact that the nanocomposite film has a lower OP value and higher UV barrier compared to the neat CS (refer to Sections 3.9.2 and 3.9.3). This behaviour can be because of the rancidification of ω-3 oils, which usually occurs from the oxidation due to their numerous double bonds, and their position within the fatty acid chain (Albert et al., 2013). Additionally, external factors like UV light can accelerate lipid oxidation, as exposure to sunlight generally facilitates the oxidation of ω-3 oil (J. Wang et al., 2021).

Furthermore, the intrinsic antioxidant property of incorporated gCD could contribute to delayed rancidification, albeit to a limited extent owing to the different properties of hydrophilic gCD and hydrophobic ω-3 oil. In conclusion, the remarkable antioxidant properties of the CS/gCD nanocomposite film were due to the combined synergistic effect of the higher oxygen content and UV barrier properties, as well as the radical scavenging properties of gCDs, as determined by the DPPH and ABTS assays.

In this study, multifunctional gCDs were synthesised from GBL via a facile hydrothermal synthesis, and then CS-based nanocomposite films were fabricated through extrusion processes. This presents an innovative fabrication method for potential industrial food-packaging applications. Incorporating gCDs into the CS film matrix led to a significant improvement in UV-light barrier properties, blocking 98.91 % in UV-B and 89.7 % in the UV-A region. Furthermore, the functional films containing gCDs exhibited antioxidant properties owing to the combined synergistic effect of oxygen and UV barrier, along with intrinsic antioxidant properties during food storage trials, indicating the potential of the CS/gCD2% film as a robust antioxidant food packaging material to extend the shelf life of ω-3 oil. These findings highlight the potential of the developed CS/gCD nanocomposite film as a packaging material that protects oily and oxygen-susceptible foods and drugs. The innovation of this study lies in the utilisation of natural waste resources as carbon precursors, which are transformed into natural nano-reinforcements and nano-antioxidants through the extrusion process. This study endeavours to pave the way for sustainable and advanced solutions in the field of food packaging, aligning with the global pursuit of environmentally friendly practices and food waste reduction.

Ji Sou Lyu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jaejoon Han: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.
